channel.title: Jornal TRIBUNA DE MACAU EN
channel.description: Jornal TRIBUNA DE MACAU EN RSS

item.title: “It is important to have applied research at the Faculty of Arts and Humanities”
item.description:
“Many things have changed” in the cultural panorama of Macau in the last decade, according to the director of the Faculty of Arts and Humanities of the University of São José. In an interview with the TRIBUNA DE MACAU newspaper, professor Carlos Sena Caires spoke about the evolution of the use of technology in the arts, the relevance of applied research in digital humanities and the role that universities play within society. In addition to wanting to expand the college's curricular offering, Carlos Sena Caires lamented the fact that there was no “preponderant artistic education route in secondary schools”. Looking ahead to the next decade, the director recognized Macao's potential to become a reference in digital media and highlighted the importance of “teaching how to recognize quality”
PEDRO MILHEIRÃO -In 2017, in an interview with the TRIBUNA DE MACAU newspaper, he said that there was a lack of vision for “less traditional cultural projects” in Macau. Do you still think the same? -This statement of mine had to do with the idea I had of Portugal. I was working at the Catholic University in Porto and we had a sound and image department that was very focused on the development of technological arts. I had already come here a few times as a visiting professor. And, in 2016, I accepted an invitation to join the University of São José (USJ), full time. I realized that the arts and technology aspect was a little lacking. Many things have changed, particularly in the cultural and artistic aspect, with groups such as “Art For All”, Fundação Oriente, with new investments in young artists from Macau, also focusing more on installations and the development of the binomial “art and technology”. When I arrived here, whenever I spoke to colleagues in the arts, I always said “there's a lack of a truly contemporary art museum here, there's a lack of a Serralves Museum”. And it seems that we are planning to have a center there, a contemporary artistic “hub” on one of these new islands. It would do Macau a lot of good to have this area not only for contemporary art exhibitions, but for research. As we have in Hong Kong, “M+”, which is also an archive center and a contemporary art museum. The fact that we have the “TeamLab” in Macau is also something relevant, from the point of view of the sensorial experience in an artistic and technological environment. Being here in Macau, with a large exhibition area, also shows that there is interest.
-Between 2014 and 2024, the number of cultural events doubled from around 40 thousand to 80 thousand. Do you think this growth represents greater artistic diversity or is there a lack of strategic vision? -Sometimes the Government lacks a kind of symbolic event. There are symbolic events, but in the symbolic event there is a “symbol”. Something that really brings thousands of people, that stands out. I remember telling a colleague, a Government representative, that in Madeira there is a big fireworks festival at the end of the year, which attracts a lot of people. But there is also the decoration of Christmas lights throughout Funchal. And every year they try to have a light installation that is iconic or grand. There is a landmark: “the largest illuminated Christmas tree in Europe”. We have to go there and take the photo. And Macau could have, in every event it holds, something unique. With the financial capacity that the Macau Government has, they could invest a little more in this. And also in quality. In this aspect, it is interesting to have quantity. But sometimes quantity dilutes quality. But, I don't think it's a problem in Macau. There is a lot of quality, there are things that are very well made in Macau.
-From local artists? -Maybe there are [more] people who bring in from outside. I'm now remembering City of Dreams, which always brings renowned artists, which is good for the city, but it's still 'pop' culture. It has its value, but I think the emergence of art is also something else. It is not only popular artistic culture, but it is also “taking risks”. New artists who are not yet established and who need to be given a hand. There are a lot of calls for local artists, a lot of calls for competition to submit works, photography, installations. There are opportunities.
-In the 2013/2014 academic year, the ratio of local students and international students, in the arts, was relatively balanced, at 48% to 52%, respectively. In the 2023/2024 school year, 13% were local and 87% foreign. Do you notice this as a teacher? -I don't know the reality of other universities. USJ represents the largest number of countries in one university in Macau, I think. We have more than 50 countries represented. The issue is that we don't have a fine arts college in Macau. It doesn't exist. And there is no predominant artistic education route in secondary schools. We have schools with an artistic focus, but where they teach how to draw, where they teach how to be creative, where they teach how to be original, from a more scientific, research angle, linked to a specific discipline which is design, which is architecture, which is the art of new media and technology. It is an issue that could be revisited. Students entering design do not have basic knowledge of drawing or descriptive geometry. Any student in Portugal who goes to these areas already comes with a 12th, an 11th, with these fundamentals.
-How does a university participate in the cultural ecosystem and what role should it play? -A fundamental aspect is to show what students do and take them to the city center, to society. We always try, every year, to have work by our students in galleries outside the university. For example, at Creative Macau, we have held an annual exhibition, “Selected Works”, where we choose the best works of finalists from all areas. We also work with casinos. We have a very close relationship with some of the casinos, where we also do expository work by teachers and students, where students also collaborate. This is the first aspect, showing the students’ work. Another is the intern component. All of our degrees in the most creative areas have a mandatory internship component. Some go to art galleries, like Creative. Others go to casinos, like the Venetian, to work in creative areas like “TeamLab”. And something that is also important, which we are working on now, is the issue of applied research, for [the benefit of] society. Universities have this fundamental role of bringing knowledge to society, to companies, to government institutions. This is an aspect that is important in our areas. But it's something that takes time.
-Do you think that the creative industries in Macau have a certain dependence on casinos? Are there any associated risks? -No. I think there are many Government initiatives that allow the development of cultural and creative industries in Macau. Of course, I don't know what the Government's contractual relationship is with the casinos. I know there has been greater demand from casinos to universities for collaboration. We are preparing specialized courses for casinos. There is a demand for learning and training. For example, the master's degree we have in communication and media, at one point, many of our students came from casinos. We are also launching a new master's degree in corporate communication, because we feel that there is a demand from casinos to train their staff in these areas. Therefore, we are also working with the market and trying to follow our own mission and vision a little, obviously.
-Do universities have freedom for risky projects, for more experimental things? -Yes of course. Creating an arts school at a private university is a risky project. The Faculty of Arts and Humanities formed in 2020, with the merger of two previous faculties. One was the Faculty of Humanities and the other was the Faculty of Creative Industries, of which I was director. I remember how difficult it was sometimes to explain what a Faculty of Creative Industries was. When it was created it was a risky project. However, times when creating new courses are always difficult. The model that is now implemented in the configuration of teaching quality does not allow launching courses from one year to the next. We have to go through an entire process that lasts, but which involves international accreditation of the courses, in our case. And we have done this accreditation with A3ES. And it takes time. It always takes three years until we have the course open. It's not like creating a company overnight. USJ has interesting risky projects, particularly in research as well. We have an applied research laboratory in the area of ​​neuroscience. Last year, we just approved an applied research center in architecture and design. We want to reinforce this idea that research is extremely important in universities, but also the relationship between what is researched, what is created at the university, and civil society. It is important to have applied research at the Faculty of Arts and Humanities. Because it is also a trend that we are seeing from Macau's financing entities, such as the Fund for the Development of Sciences and Technology. They want patents, they want marketable products.
-What are the biggest projects for the coming time? -We already have almost six years to live. The first stage of the college was to try to consolidate its master areas, the creative ones. For two years we have been working to consolidate these areas. Afterwards, we reviewed the structure of the faculty and its departments and created a new structure. Then we had some time, quite a bit, to review the programs and accredit our programs. This was very slow, it took some time and we are still in that process. There was no such tradition in Macau of course accreditation and we had to learn. Now, we are looking to create new courses. We have the University and its departments consolidated, the courses revised and, for the most part, accredited. Some are still in the process, such as master's degrees in Design and Architecture. We hope to complete accreditation in the 2026-2027 academic year and we want to launch new areas and doctorates. We are proposing a doctorate in design, which has already been approved internally, and now we will submit it to external bodies for accreditation, and we plan, in the future, to launch two more doctorates, one in the area of ​​media art and technology and the other in the area of ​​architecture. And we also have two new master's degrees, which are already in the accreditation process, waiting for the agency's final report. One is in the area of ​​corporate communications and the other is a master's degree in media art and technology, expected to open next year.
-Do you believe that Macau can be a reference in digital areas? -I think so. In digital areas, this is where we want to go, especially because one of the college's visions or missions is to be a college of excellence in digital humanities. We see that there are major developments at the University of Macau, there are new areas that are emerging, it seems to me that they also want to launch architecture. There is the big “international hub” project in Hengqin. Other interesting things are being developed. It is great for Macau to have these opportunities.
-We started the interview looking at the last 10 years. Looking at the next 10, if you could make one structural decision for culture in Macau, what would it be? -Teach how to recognize quality. What is quality or what is good taste, according to Asian standards, of course. It was important to form society for quality. Because standardization is the enemy of excellence. We have to value what is done well and teach people to learn and understand what is good. It's a challenge when I teach my students in the area of ​​design. My challenge is to teach them how to see a product, it could be a poster, a piece, and know why it has quality. And that is a challenge that takes years, teaching these generations to have a sensitive eye for quality. There must be this commitment in Macau, to know how to teach how to understand the aesthetics of beauty, what “beautiful” is in the philosophical sense, and what a quality product is. I feel that in the consumer society we live in, hyper-consumerist, quality is sometimes lost, diluted. A lot is done by trivialization.
-A very common argument is that beauty is subjective. -No. You can teach the principles of aesthetics, composition, design. There are principles that a person learns in their training and then reverts to a critical look at things. If a person is aware of these principles, they will realize that there is some qualitative value there that needs to be highlighted and deserves to be said. "I think this is beautiful. It's my opinion, so it prevails." I don't think it's that way, on the contrary. As a higher education institution, we need to teach these values, aesthetics and morals. It's something that is fundamental for us, otherwise we wouldn't be here. The banality I was talking about seems to me to be very inimical to quality. And this argument that everything is subjective and everything is individualizable doesn't seem to me to be very correct.

item.title: Increased labor rights could be “devastating for some SMEs”
item.description:
Helena Kok, a lawyer specializing in Labor Law, argues that the proposals to review the Labor Relations Law are few, “but significant”. In an article published on the website of the law firm MdME and on the Lexology platform, the lawyer considers that, “although increasing rights may be viable for large employers, it could be devastating for some small and medium-sized companies”. In the “not so promising” business environment in which SMEs “currently navigate”, there are several “priority” questions that need answers, he considers
VÍTOR REBELO
The consequences of the changes to the Labor Relations Law, which are under public consultation until March 16, could create major problems for small and medium-sized companies (SMEs). The person defending this is lawyer Helena Kok, in an article published on the website of the law firm MdME and on the Lexology platform, which describes itself as the main global legal intelligence platform for mitigating risks and finding specialized advice. The author recalls that, for these changes, specifically relating to maternity leave and annual leave, the Government commissioned a feasibility study from a third party. “Judging by the conclusions presented in the consultation document, I can assume that this is a very formal inquiry”, he says, emphasizing that it is “obvious that employees will ask for more and employers will ask for less”. In the analysis, the lawyer, with specialization in the area of ​​Labor Law, states that “although increasing rights may be viable for large employers, it can be devastating for some SMEs”. Looking at a traditional Macau company with 10 employees, each with an average of 10 years of service, he notes that, according to the proposal, “almost all of them would see their annual leave double almost overnight”, which “contrasts strongly with the ‘progressive’ intention of the structure”. "Given that SMEs represent 99% of Macau's companies, the public report of the study does not address the following questions: How many SMEs would face this sudden doubling? How many would be forced to increase the number of employees to fill the gaps? How many would simply not survive the increase in labor costs?", he argues. Furthermore, he continues, the study “does not appear to have investigated how many employers might – covertly – change their hiring practices for women of childbearing age if maternity leave was increased to 80, 90 or 120 days.” In the article, Helena Kok adds that, “although it is very likely that the government subsidy (the same as the last change from 56 to 70 days in 2020) will be implemented as a transitional relief, these issues remain very relevant”, admitting that “the conclusions would shape not only the duration of the leave, but also the support structure”. “For example, should the proposal also include the creation of an independent commission to combat discrimination or an ombudsman to monitor and penalize discriminatory behavior against women as soon as the new law comes into force?”, he continues. In the “not so promising” business environment in which SMEs “currently navigate”, these are the “priority questions that need answers”, he reflects. Helena Kok predicts that these changes will be approved largely as proposed. “My logic is based on pragmatism and timing”, he continues. First, “the proposal has little room for maneuver, since adjusting the numbers up or down now would create unnecessary waves of discontent on both sides of the stakeholder.” Secondly, “this change is the first item of an ambitious government agenda for 2026, and other equally important and sensitive changes to workers' rights are in preparation”, such as maximum compensation for work accidents and occupational diseases, and maximum compensation for unilateral termination of the employment contract. He admits that, “to comply with the legislative calendar, there will be no desire to carry out further studies or new consultations before the proposed law enters the Legislative Assembly for discussion, normally long, among the members of the Permanent Commission”. Therefore, he emphasizes, “the future is set”, highlighting that, for Human Resources leaders, the most prudent path is to start preparing, preparing personnel proposals, updating templates for employment contracts and employee manuals and giving instructions to their suppliers to configure Human Resources information systems for the new rules, he argues. “The question isn’t really ‘if.’ It’s ‘is it ready?’”, asks the author of the article.
Revision of the law creates expectations The public consultation is organized by the Directorate of Labor Affairs Services, with the aim of collecting opinions on improving legislation, with a view to improving working conditions and adapting the law to the current needs of society. The first proposed change is to increase the minimum maternity leave from 70 to 90 days, with 30 flexible days before childbirth and 60 mandatory days immediately after. The second focuses on the minimum annual leave from a fixed regime of six days to a progressive regime, adding one day for every two years of service, up to a maximum limit of 12 days. In the published article, Helena Kok says that “the city is, rightly, in an uproar over the debate on this topic”, a debate that led her to listen to the voices of interested parties and analyze practices around the world. “In strategic management, strategists seek to maximize performance with minimal resources and thus create or reinforce a competitive advantage,” he writes. On the other hand, “improving employees’ rest time improves the balance between professional and personal life, promotes a greater sense of belonging and job stability”, he highlights. “These are often presented as opposing interests”, he says, asking the question: “Are they really incompatible?” Launching the phrase “the long road to equal rights for an equal gender”, the lawyer states that low birth rates have become an urgent issue in Macau, with the UN reporting a fertility level in the city of just 0.68 births per woman – the lowest in a group of 48 countries or regions that are expected to reach their population peak before 2054 or within 30 years. “Although many factors are at play, unattractive protections for women in the private sector are, without a doubt, one of the main contributors”, he observes, recalling that “for decades, a 90-day maternity leave has been the minimum standard for public servants, while their colleagues in the private sector have been treated as second-class citizens, with much less”, being in his opinion, an “unfair disparity that should have long been corrected”. As childbirth is a “natural experience exclusive to women”, the need for postpartum rest is medically indisputable, applying to all women, regardless of whether their employer is the Government or a small company”. “Differentiated minimum protection is in no way justifiable”, he explains. In purely mathematical terms, “it took more than a decade to begin to reduce this difference”. The MdME lawyer emphasizes that, in general, the proposals included in the law review, “although few”, are significant and that employment is a “socially sensitive issue that requires a holistic view”. It also points out that if Generation Z workers “do not have meaningful breaks to socialize, meet partners or see their families, both the workplace and society will be at great risk of becoming toxic”.

item.title: MP wants Macau as legal interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries
item.description:
The Attorney General of Macau, Tong Hio Fong, pointed out that the Public Ministry (MP) will play the role of jurisdictional interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries. During the “Two Sessions” taking place in Beijing, the Central Government once again highlighted Macau's strategic role in connecting with Portuguese-speaking countries. The work report presented by the Prime Minister, Li Qiang, highlighted that the region must enhance its unique advantages and deepen its role as an interconnection platform between Mainland China and the Portuguese-speaking space, in order to promote “long-term prosperity and stability”. As highlighted by the MP, in a statement reacting to the “Two Sessions”, the Macao SAR will be consolidated as a platform for judicial cooperation and professional communication, in order to bring legal systems closer together and promote exchanges between magistrates and institutions in Portuguese-speaking countries. The same organization intends to deepen collaboration in the Greater Bay Area and in the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin, but always with the perspective that Macau should serve as a “bridgehead” for China's opening to the Portuguese-speaking world. According to Tong, the objective is for Macau to increasingly assert itself as a “privileged link between China and the Portuguese-speaking international community”, contributing to national development and consolidating its strategic position within the “one country, two systems” policy. Macau's government plans for 2026 include the signing of an agreement for mutual legal assistance in criminal matters with Angola. According to the Government Action Lines, Macau also wants to start negotiating an “agreement on the reciprocal confirmation and execution of judicial decisions in civil and commercial matters” with Portugal. However, the document does not mention any date for the conclusion of negotiations and the eventual signing of an agreement after all. Macau and Portugal signed an agreement in 2019 regarding the surrender of fugitive offenders, whose criminal legality was called into question by the Portuguese Bar Association. The protocol is not in force, as it was not voted on in the Assembly of the Republic.
PRC courts handled more than 31 thousand cases regarding SAR and Taiwan Last year, China's courts concluded more than 31 thousand cases related to Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan, said the president of the Supreme People's Court, Zhang Jun, in the presentation of the annual work report, at the National People's Assembly (ANP), and cited by Radio Macau. According to him, the volume of completed processes represents an annual increase of around 16%. Zhang Jun also indicated that the Guangdong courts invited mediation organizations from Hong Kong and Macau to mediate around a thousand cases of external disputes with the SARs. And he explained that arbitration awards were enforced in Mainland China in cases where companies established in the nine cities of the Greater Bay Area chose Hong Kong or Macau as the arbitration location. The chairman of the ANP Standing Committee, Zhao Leji, also presented the work report, saying that in 2026 greater emphasis will be given to the coordinated advancement of the rule of law, together with reform, development and stability.
JTM with Lusa

item.title: Environmental Forum highlights innovative results in green technologies
item.description:
This year, the International Environmental Cooperation Forum and Exhibition will present innovative results in green technologies, related to different sectors and everyday application scenarios. One of the new features of MIECF is the first dry paper pulp recycling system in Macau. A platform for trading second-hand items will also be launched, committed to actively promoting a “zero waste” lifestyle. The organization anticipates an increase in exhibitors
VÍTOR REBELO
The 2026 edition of the International Environmental Cooperation Forum and Exhibition (MIECF), which will take place at the Venetian's Cotai Expo, between March 26th and 28th, will present, in a concentrated way, innovative results in green technologies, related to different sectors and application scenarios in everyday life. Among them, we highlight the first dry paper pulp recycling system in Macau, ecological and antibacterial packaging for meals made from rice husks and a second-hand goods trading platform, launched for the first time in the territory and committed to actively promoting a “zero waste” lifestyle. Furthermore, this year the MIECF will unveil the world's first carbon capture compartment, the technology of autoclaved aerated concrete blocks with carbon fixation, applied in the first “fast food” chain with a zero carbon footprint in Mainland China. These technologies aim to “promote the sustainable and high-quality development of the sector, while continually enriching the day-to-day application scenarios of green technology”, according to a statement, which adds that greater international participation of traders and buyers is expected than in the previous year, generating opportunities for green cooperation. The event continues to deepen environmental cooperation at a regional and international level, bringing together giant international companies in the energy sector, classified in the Fortune Global 500, reference companies in the environmental sector in Mainland China, Portuguese-speaking environmental organizations, leading technology companies and exhibitors of top brands from different countries and regions. During the fair, under the theme “Low Carbon and Zero Waste Cities: Embarking on a Global Collaboration”, exhibitors will have the possibility to participate in the 2nd Global Carbon Credit Market Development Forum, in thematic contact scholarship sessions, green traveling exhibitions, green workshops, among other activities. Covering areas such as the circular economy, waste management and green construction, the MIECF brings together cutting-edge environmental information and business opportunities at a global level, with the aim of “boosting industrial modernization through the application of green technologies”, highlights the organization. The Exhibition Zone alluding to the Circular Economy with Zero Waste, opened this year, displays the most recent results in the areas of waste management, resource recycling, innovation for zero waste and green construction, among others. Furthermore, in response to the Government Action Lines for the promotion of ecological vehicles, the fair also has a Green Mobility Exhibition Zone, focusing on new energy vehicles, charging infrastructures and green mobility solutions. In turn, the Green and Low Carbon Projects Zone presents carbon neutrality strategies, ESG (Environmental, Social and Governance) criteria, as well as case studies in green investment, promoting green innovation, industrial transformation and modernization.

item.title: Seguro wants to stop “electoral frenzy”
item.description:
epa12806036 The new Portuguese President Antonio Jose Seguro (2-L), flanked by the parliament president, Jose Pedro Aguiar Branco (2-R), and the former President, Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa (R), delivers his first speech during the swearing-in ceremony in the Portuguese parliament in Lisbon, Portugal, 09 March 2026. Antonio Jose Seguro, former secretary-general of the Portuguese Socialist Party, was elected President of the Republic in the second round of the presidential elections on 8 February 2026, receiving over 3.5 million votes, a record number, representing 66.84 percent of the vote cast, against Andre Ventura, president of Chega. EPA/JOSE SENA GOULAO
The new President of the Portuguese Republic stated yesterday that he will do everything to stop the “electoral frenzy” and asked parties with parliamentary representation for “a clear political commitment” for stability. In his inauguration speech, at the Assembly of the Republic, António José Seguro considered that, having finished “an electoral cycle of three elections and four trips to the polls in just new months”, Portugal has “a golden opportunity” to find “durable solutions” in a “new cycle of three years without national elections”.
epa12806015 The new Portuguese President Antonio Jose Seguro (L), flanked by the parliament president, Jose Pedro Aguiar Branco (R), greets the former President, Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa (C), during the swearing in ceremony in the Portuguese parliament in Lisbon, Portugal, 09 March 2026. Antonio Jose Seguro, former secretary-general of the Portuguese Socialist Party, was elected President of the Republic in the second round of the presidential elections on 8 February 2026, receiving over 3.5 million votes, a record number, representing 66.84 percent of the vote cast, against Andre Ventura, president of Chega. EPA/JOSE SENA GOULAO
The new head of state defended that the challenges facing the country make it inadvisable for “an electoral calendar of selfish convenience”, adding: “The experience of the recent past, of two-year electoral cycles, is not desirable. I will do everything to stop this electoral frenzy”. Seguro reiterated that “the rejection of the proposed State Budget law does not automatically imply the dissolution of the Assembly of the Republic”. “Legislatures are to comply and we must all assume this responsibility, Government and oppositions”, he declared. To the Government headed by Luís Montenegro, the President promised “loyal and fruitful cooperation” and to the parties he assured that he would treat “everyone equally”. As the country's “structural challenges”, he pointed out “insufficient economic growth, an economy based on low wages, persistent inequalities, constant poverty, demographic aging, delays in justice, public bureaucracies, difficulties in accessing healthcare and housing”. “Lack of labor, scarcity of opportunities for younger people, insecurity for older people, distrust in institutions and politics”, he added, commenting: “Unfortunately, a list that is too long and cumbersome”. He also highlighted the “Herculean effort” that will be necessary for the “reconstruction and recovery of a significant part of the national territory devastated by this winter's catastrophes”. “Let’s be clear: none of these challenges can be resolved with improvisation, with goals that are immediately exhausted and aimed exclusively at an electoral calendar of selfish convenience,” he added. The President asked the parties for “a clear political commitment, with contributions from the largest possible number of political parties, so that democratic stability, predictability in public policies, governing capacity and focus on urgent responses and structural reforms are guaranteed”. He argued that “only then will the country be viable” and that he is not asking for “artificial unanimity” nor for “ideological differences” to be erased. "I'm talking about democratic maturity. I intend to place the national interest above short-term logic and electoral interests. This commitment is not against democracy. It is for democracy", he countered. On the other hand, he warned that Portugal is not immune to threats to the pillars of the democratic system, which it established as “red lines”, assuming the task of “taking care of democracy”. "I want to make it clear that stability is not an end in itself, much less does it mean stagnation and immobility. Stability is a condition for change, not a goal", he stated, warning that "recent history reveals that in a very short time what was built over centuries is being destroyed and that few are demolishing a civilizational landmark resulting from the contributions of many". Regarding the intention to obtain an “inter-party commitment” on health, announced during the campaign, he said that he will soon invite the parties to begin this process, to “guarantee access to health, and in a sustained way safeguard the continuity of the National Health Service”. Seguro wants “a commitment that goes beyond government cycles, with the involvement of all actors in the health sector, with goals, policies, measures, multi-annual budgets and evaluation of results”. “The same would be achieved in other essential areas such as, for example, access to housing, population rejuvenation, creation of better opportunities for young people, faster justice, an efficient State, economic growth based on a model with better wages, with equality criteria that put an end to unacceptable wage discrimination against Portuguese women”, he said.
Thanks to Marcelo At the beginning of his speech, Seguro thanked his predecessor for his dedication to Portugal and expressed respect for the plurality of parliament and assuring him institutional cooperation. He also thanked the Portuguese for the trust they placed in him and promised that he will be “President of all of Portugal and President of all Portuguese, whether they live in Portugal or abroad”. Addressing Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa, he left him a “word of gratitude for his dedication to Portugal and the defense of the national interest” and expressed to him “the affection of a country that has always felt his presence”, considering that, “whatever the assessment that each one makes of their mandates, no one can deny him the love for Portugal”. “As Jorge de Sena wrote, Portugal is made up of those who leave and those who stay – a feeling that President Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa interpreted so well when, innovating, he decided to hold the celebrations of Portugal Day, in the national territory and in the diaspora; a practice that I decided to continue, as we share the same interpretation”, he added. “Also on these grounds, I decided to award him the highest degree of the Order of Liberty – the great collar of the Order of Liberty – in a ceremony to take place today”, he highlighted. Before the ceremony, when he was walking down São Bento Street to go to the Assembly of the Republic for Seguro's inauguration, Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa stated that his successor will be “a great President” and “must count on the support of all Portuguese”. Seguro also welcomed the former presidents of the Republic Aníbal Cavaco Silva, “whose presence represents a living sign of the institutional continuity” of Portuguese democracy, and António Ramalho Eanes “who, for reasonable reasons, was unable to join this session”, and evoked the memory of Mário Soares and Jorge Sampaio. "In addition to the different political interpretations that may exist in relation to my predecessors, there remains recognition for the services they provided to Portugal, which indelibly mark the history of our democratic life. The legacy they leave us is one of the greatest assets of our democracy", he considered.
PS available for appointments The secretary general of the PS expressed full agreement with the content of the inauguration speech of the new President of the Republic and highlighted that the lack of political commitments, until now, was not due to the unavailability of the socialists. In front of journalists, José Luís Carneiro argued that between the President of the Republic and the PS there is a coincidence of positions in defense of “political stability that corresponds to options that serve fundamental basic needs”, particularly in terms of sovereign functions, an effective State in the internal order and based on international law, on the Charter of the United Nations”. “We need a State that puts political commitments at the service of policies: responses in health, housing, wages and living conditions, particularly for the younger generations. These are the political priorities since I took office as general secretary of the PS", he argued. In turn, the PSD parliamentary leader argued that António José Seguro had a "message very aligned" with that of his party and the Government, considering stability a means to "transform people's lives". needs many. Many of them are being done, but they need time”, said Hugo Soares.
JTM with Lusa

item.title: DST promoted MSAR in the United Kingdom
item.description:
The Directorate of Tourism Services sent a delegation to London, led by Maria Helena de Senna Fernandes, to hold a promotional seminar on Macau, with tourism partners and the UK media. The promotional action also included the collaboration of Pal Lok Chok, director of the group “MyLand Culture”, and local artist Filipe Dores
More than 100 representatives from the tourism industries of Macau and the United Kingdom were in London to participate in a seminar promoting Macau as a tourist destination, according to the Tourism Services Bureau (DST). The initiative aimed to expand the visitor market from Europe, presenting “Macao's latest tourism elements” and “multi-destination” tourism products to British partners and media outlets, with the support of the Chinese National Tourism Office in London. Pal Lok Chok, director of the artistic and cultural group “MyLand Culture”, presented Macau's cultural heritage, as well as artistic projects that integrate art and community. In turn, local artist Filipe Dores shared his experience of transforming tea culture “into a cultural practice of contemporary art”. The event also presented the themed painting “Timeless Voyage”, which was inspired by Macau as “an important node on the Maritime Silk Road”, said the statement. In parallel, DST collaborated with Harry Jaggard, a British content creator with more than 9.5 million followers who was in Macau, Hong Kong and Shenzhen last month. In addition, the seminar featured different creative and cultural elements, such as the special “Macao” cocktail, the distribution of lucky bags with souvenirs from the partnership between DST and “MyLand”, the exhibition of limited edition works and the promotion of community tours in Macau. On the occasion, Maria Helena de Senna Fernandes highlighted the “more convenient conditions for visitors from the United Kingdom to travel together to Macau, Mainland China and the Greater Bay Area”, in force since February, when the visa-free policy for China began to include British visitors. The director of DST also highlighted that the organization intended to “showcase the dynamism of ‘tourism+community+culture’ in Macau” and “strengthen cooperation with tour operators in the United Kingdom”, in order to offer visitors to the European country “a rich and diverse tourist experience in Macau”. For his part, Zhang Li, director of the National Tourism Office of China in London, stated that “he will fully take advantage of the policy of unilateral visa exemption for British citizens, to build a more fluid exchange bridge, attract more foreign visitors for tourist visits, commercial cooperation, research and academic exchange in the Greater Bay Area”, in favor of “a better future for Macau”.
P.M.

item.title: Painter shows historical encounters between cultures
item.description:
Works by painter Gao Lipeng will be on display from today at the Rui Cunha Foundation Gallery, in an exhibition that includes 26 Chinese ink works. The paintings depict the artist's walks through the city's streets and alleys.
The Rui Cunha Foundation (FRC) opens today, at 6:30 pm, the Solo Exhibition “Mirror Heart”, Artistic Journey by Gao Lipeng, a painter active in the artistic world of Macau. The exhibition includes 26 works in Chinese ink and pastel tones. Between originals and replicas, the works, which will be on view until March 21st, were created by the artist on his walks through the streets and alleys of Macau, during the last three years, “where he got lost and took time trying to capture the texture and living history of the city”, according to an introductory note from the FRC. The selection, from among more than three hundred pieces, was made by curators Jun Zilan and Lalin, under the academic guidance of Wang Lan. The set reveals a choice of the painter's most representative works, which demonstrate his “spiritual reconstruction” of the history and cultural landscape of Macau. Gao Lipeng's art integrates “meticulous traditional Chinese brushwork, with contemporary artistic perspectives, reflecting his deep involvement with cultural heritage and urban narratives, particularly through his thematic works on Macau”, states the exhibition manifesto. He adds that the painter's art “embodies a fusion of academic rigor and creative innovation, establishing him as a prominent figure in contemporary Chinese painting.” The public who visits the exhibition can appreciate, through the architectural texture and the layers of light and shadow of the works on display, “the historical encounters” between Eastern and Western cultures captured by the artist. According to Wang Lan, the exhibition summarizes Gao Lipeng's academic phase in Macau and interprets his quest to “see the heart through art”. In the dialogue between tradition and modernity, “he uses paint to address contemporary themes of urban landscapes, paving a visual path for viewers that connects personal experiences to collective memory”, he highlights. Gao Lipeng is a member of the China Artists Association, a visiting researcher at Peking University, and a board member of the Peking University Alumni Calligraphy and Painting Association. The co-organization of the exhibition is the FRC, together with Publicação Macau Man Ngai, Macau Association of Arts and Humanities, Shandong Cultural Exchange Association and Fraternal Association of Weifang Countrymen.
V.R.

item.title: Suggested measures to accelerate integration with Hengqin
item.description: The integration between Macau and Hengqin was one of the topics in focus, with several members of the National People's Assembly (ANP) and Macau representatives in the National Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC) presenting proposals on the matter. According to Radio Macau, Vong Hin Fai, a Macau member of the APN, suggested that the Governments of the MSAR, Guangdong and Zhuhai take advantage of their respective legislative powers to resolve institutional issues. Meanwhile, José Chui Sai Peng proposed the creation of “Macao Standard applicable in Hengqin” mechanisms in the financial area to allow local professionals to provide services in the Macau New District of Hengqin. Kevin Ho, also a Macau member of the ANP, advocated promoting the “Guangzhou Manufacturing + Macau Services” model, encouraging Macau's professional services institutions to establish branches in free trade zones such as Nansha. Furthermore, it considers that a “New National Pilot Zone for Maritime Opening Economy” should be created in Guangzhou. In turn, Ho Ion Sang, permanent member of the CPPCC National Committee, suggested expanding the use of safe conduct and the progressive integration of residents' identification documents, including BIR, safe conduct and residence certificate. Wong Kit Cheng, in turn, defended the simplification of the recognition of professional qualifications, while Chan Hong said he hoped that the use of Macau medicines could be expanded in the Guangdong and Macau Traditional Chinese Medicine Scientific and Industrial Park.

item.title: European ‘think tank’ once again distinguished CTM
item.description: The Macau Telecommunications Company (CTM) won the “Business Innovation Driven by Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Premium User Experience” award for the second year in a row, according to a press release. Awarded by “IDATE”, a leading think tank in Europe, the distinction is a recognition of “pioneering efforts in advancing AI-driven innovation in network and business operations, alongside exceptional service excellence”. Ebel Cham, vice-president of the company's commercial operations, was present at the “Mobile World Congress”, in Barcelona, ​​to receive the award. On the occasion, he emphasized the role of CTM as a “key facilitator of intelligent transformation in different sectors of Macau” and the consistent prioritization of the exploration of AI and ‘big data’ applications. He also mentioned that CTM has been actively advancing internal digitalization by integrating AI into network operations and business services, “delivering cutting-edge, diversified and highly reliable integrated communication solutions to Macau”. Ebel Cham said the award “underlines and validates CTM’s unwavering commitment to driving AI-powered innovation and excellence.” He also highlighted the deployment of the 10-gigabit dual network infrastructure last year, which delivered “more advanced, secure and exceptional” experiences to customers and a “strong foundation to empower different sectors in Macau”. Looking to the future, CTM will remain committed to introducing cutting-edge solutions and technologies, in order to “realize the vision of an ‘AI Macau’”, guaranteed Ebel Cham.

item.title: BRIEF
item.description:
14 thousand have not yet taken proof of life In 2026, there are around 177 thousand beneficiaries who receive the elderly pension or disability pension, of which around 14 thousand have not yet provided proof of life. In this sense, the Social Security Fund (FSS) reminds beneficiaries that they can do so through electronic means, such as in the Single Account. As for beneficiaries who do not provide proof of life by the end of March, the respective benefits will no longer be granted from April onwards, warns the FSS.
SAFP create column “Together in Reform” The Administration and Public Service Services (SAFP) began to make available on its website a column entitled “Together in Reform”, to “further reinforce the transparency of the Public Administration reform and the participation of society”. According to a statement, the initiative aims to “transmit information about the reform to the public and public service workers in a timely manner, bringing together consensus and social commitment to, together, boost the modernization of the Macao SAR’s system and governance capacity”. In this space, the SAFP will disseminate “the latest advances, policy information and work status” related to the Administration reform.
Plans accept applications for scrapping old vehicles The Directorate of Environmental Protection Services (DSPA) continues to accept applications for the “Plan for granting financial support for the scrapping of gasoline motorcycles and their replacement with new electric motorcycles” and for the third phase of the “Financial support plan for the scrapping of old diesel-powered vehicles”. As the 2026 circulation tax must be paid by March 31, the organization calls on owners of eligible vehicles to submit their application as soon as possible, preferably by March 15. DSPA will then work to complete the approval and allow the vehicles to be delivered, thus being exempt from paying tax on these vehicles.

item.title: Greater inspection of tobacco and similar products planned
item.description:
A public consultation is underway on the review of legislation relating to smoking, until April 8th. The measures proposed by the Government aim to illegalize the possession of electronic cigarettes in public spaces and expand public smoking-free areas. As preventive policies, the remaining measures aim at greater supervision of new products and a new packaging regime for products containing tobacco. In addition, the Executive is also considering equipping inspection agents with audio and image recording devices
PEDRO MILHEIRÃO The Government intends to ban the possession of electronic cigarettes in public spaces, according to the Health Services (SSM) public consultation document on the “Amendment to the Smoking Prevention and Control Regime”. Ownership will cover “all components of electronic cigarettes”, such as the device, the e-liquid and the capsule, among others. The consumption of these products in public spaces “will be considered [as] possession of them” and illegal, the document states. The SSM justify this measure by the fact that the acquisition of electronic cigarettes in neighboring regions is easy and the detection of these products is difficult. Since the electronic cigarette is “small in size, easy to hide” and leaves “no visible signs of consumption (…) the entire process is highly hidden and difficult to detect immediately”, explains the organization. It should be remembered that the sale of electronic cigarettes has been prohibited since January 1, 2018, and the manufacture, distribution, sale, import, export and transport of these cigarettes were banned on December 5, 2022, in order to “allow the consumption of these previously purchased products”. In recent years, in neighboring regions and countries, electronic cigarettes have served as a “means for transporting illicit substances”, such as “etomidate”, say the SSM. In Hong Kong, possession of electronic cigarettes in public places will be prohibited from April 30th this year. On another point, the authorities intend to expand the areas where smoking is prohibited outdoors, creating new prohibition zones “in certain streets or squares with a large concentration of people”, which will be designated by order from the Chief Executive. In these areas, smoking will only be permitted in “smoking areas”. Taking into account “the reality of Macau, where spaces on public roads are limited”, and “the minimization of impacts caused to non-smokers”, the SSM anticipate a “reasonable organization”. Furthermore, the Executive intends that “entrances and exits to schools, daycare centers, among other establishments, are integrated into places where smoking is prohibited, provided for in the ‘Tobacco Control Law’, in order to protect the health of students and children”. The objective is to “effectively resolve the issue of second-hand smoke in the entry and exit points of these establishments”, argue the SSM. In the first quarter of 2026, the SSM implemented an experimental smoking ban area in the streets surrounding Parque Dr. Carlos d’ Assumpção. In November last year, the measure prohibiting smoking in the entrances of several educational establishments was also implemented. Although the ban is already in force in certain places, namely bus stops, gardens, beaches and schools, “smoking is still permitted in open spaces, such as streets and squares, so the public eventually comes into contact with secondhand smoke in the outdoor environment”, state the SSM. According to the organization, “the public is particularly concerned about the act of smoking while walking” and “the harm caused by secondhand smoke at the entrances and exits” of schools and daycare centers.
Prevent consumption of new products As a preventive solution, the document also suggests a ban on the manufacture, distribution, marketing, import and export and transport to the interior or exterior of nicotine pouches, herbal cigarettes, tobacco or water pipe paste, hookahs and their components, as well as “similar products”. Although they are not yet widespread in Macau, the SSM anticipate that “these products may reach a significant scale of consumption”. Although water pipes are regulated by the “Tobacco Control Law”, which prohibits their consumption in closed public spaces, “there has been an increase in their popularity among young people globally”, since “they can be shared by several people at the same time”, which leads them to be “often consumed on social occasions outdoors”. On the other hand, the legislation in force still “does not cover nicotine pouches or herbal cigarettes that do not contain tobacco”. “In recent years, new types of tobacco, nicotine products and tobacco substitutes have constantly appeared on the market”, observe the SSM, “with the aim of attracting a greater number of new young consumers”. These new products are often advertised as “tobacco-free”, “harm-free” and “natural”, influencing young non-smokers. Taking into account that “tobacco product packaging constitutes the main means of information with which consumers have the greatest interaction”, the authorities intend to introduce a new standardized packaging regime for tobacco products, in line with the guidelines of the World Health Organization. This regime provides for “an increase in the area allocated to labels and images of health warnings on all tobacco product packaging”, to “85% of the surface of the main face of the packaging”, indicates the document. However, “to ensure an orderly transition without obstacles in the market, it is recommended that an additional period of one year be allocated, counting from the date of publication of the law, for the aforementioned provisions to enter into force after the end of that period”.
Inspection agents with portable cameras Another of the Government's intentions is to provide inspection agents, when carrying out their duties and under legal terms, with “portable recording devices, such as cameras”, similar to what has been happening in Hong Kong since August last year. This measure aims to ensure the legal protection of agents, “in situations of threat of physical conflict”. Recording devices must be used “visibly, attached to their uniform”, ensuring “always open and transparent” use and reserved for episodes in which agents “face threats or resistance during law enforcement”. Furthermore, agents will be obliged to inform the individual who will be the subject of recording in advance, before proceeding to capture images or sounds, and end it “as soon as the threats or resistance cease”. Furthermore, the portable recording device “will emit a visual notification, such as an indicator light, during the recording process”, allowing the person in question to be aware of the recordings, guaranteeing “the public's right to information and access to it”. The records will be stored “for the sole purpose of proving the occurrence of a threat or resistance during the law enforcement process”, and “all data will be deleted seven days after the date of recording, with the aim of guaranteeing the preservation of personal data in an adequate manner”, refer to the SSM. The consultation period began yesterday and will continue until April 8th, and the SSM will prepare the final report within 90 days after the end of the public consultation. Yesterday, a consultation session took place aimed at the industrial, commercial and tourist sectors. Another session is scheduled for March 14th, with civic associations, while a third will be held on March 19th, aimed at the public.

item.title: Predominance of executive power requires population and sectoral support
item.description:
Governing the special administrative regions based on the predominance of executive power “is not an easy task”, noted the Deputy Prime Minister, Ding Xuexiang, which is why he argued that it is not just a responsibility of the leaders of Macau and Hong Kong. Quoted by “Ou Mun Tin Toi”, the also member of the Standing Committee of the Politburo of the Communist Party of China argued that the predominance of the executive power requires the cooperation and support of all sectors of society, including the legislative and judicial powers. In a joint meeting with members from Hong Kong and Macao at the National Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference, Ding Xuexiang emphasized that executive-led governance is not only the responsibility of the Chief Executive, but also of the SAR governments, the general public and all sectors of society. More than 200 people attended the meeting, including Xia Baolong, director of the State Council's SAR Affairs Office. On the occasion, Ding Xuexiang also stressed that the SARs must take advantage of the main opportunities presented by the 15th Five-Year Plan and take “solid measures” to promote “high-quality” economic development. He also asked to inspire more people in Hong Kong and Macau to “love both the country and its regions”, in addition to promoting the integration of the SARs into the country's overall development. In a meeting with deputies from the SAR to the National People's Assembly (ANP), the deputy PM proposed to “firmly promote the adequate diversification of the economy, further advance the construction of the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin, and effectively leverage the boosted effect of industrial clusters”, as well as “continuously highlight the role of ‘a center, a platform and a base’”, according to Chinese radio. Praising the work of the ANP deputies, he also expressed hope that they will continue to perform their duties diligently and achieve results, fully supporting the SAR Government, “uniting all sectors of society and jointly promoting the prosperity and development of Hong Kong and Macau to a new level”.
Kou Hoi In talks about new objectives for the Executive In turn, Kou Hoi In, member of the ANP Standing Committee and former president of the Macau Legislative Assembly, stated that the 15th Five-Year Plan presented by the Central Government clearly defines new objectives for the Executives of the Macao SAR and Hong Kong. According to him, according to the new guidelines, in the coming years, Beijing wants a greater focus on proactive measures to increase government effectiveness. Therefore, the Macao SAR Executive must increase transparency and raise the level of professionalization to reinforce society's trust. Kou Hoi In also stressed that the territory must improve institutional coordination, invest in the training of qualified staff and effectively use digital and intelligent resources. Furthermore, he pointed out that integration in the construction of the Greater Bay Area and the advancement of the development of the Deep Cooperation Zone are two fundamental points for Macau to assume a more relevant role in the context of national development. On the other hand, Kou Hoi In argued that, in an international context marked by complexity and instability, the SARs have the advantage of being platforms for connecting with the world. In this sense, they can help to expand the country's international cooperation space and deepen cultural exchanges.
Chief reiterates that state security law is a constitutional duty The Chief Executive reiterated that the law relating to the defense of State security is a constitutional responsibility of Macau, in an interview with China News Service. Quoted by TDM, based on news from “Ou Mun Tin Toi”, Sam Hou Fai also considered that the stable and long-term application of the “one country, two systems” principle will depend on the legal mechanisms provided for in the new law. Stressing that national security legislation constitutes a constitutional duty and a prerequisite to ensure the stable and long-term implementation of the “one country, two systems” principle, Sam Hou Fai also argued that the provisions relating to the national security system, mechanisms and legal framework provide fundamental guarantees for the territory to respond to the complex external environment. Therefore, it considers that the constant improvement of the law is fundamental for the correct implementation of the Basic Law.
Economic diversification seen as “essential” for Macau’s future Leonel Alves, Macau's representative at the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference, told TDM that economic diversification is “essential” to “guarantee Macau's future as a special administrative region”. In Beijing for the “Two Sessions”, the Macanese addressed the topics in focus: “The world is very troubled, China is in full economic and social development, it has many objectives to achieve, the main topic that arises is the 15th Five-Year Plan and the inclusion of Macau in the country's development plan”. Leonel Alves stressed that these issues are relevant “for all communities in Macau”, because, he added, “China's progress also implies Macau's progress, China's stability is also Macau's stability”. Chan Hong, deputy for Macau to the National People's Assembly, defended TDM that the Macao SAR should make a greater contribution to national development.
CP.

item.title: Macao SAR bodies promise to implement “spirit” of the “Two Sessions”
item.description:
epa12795922 A general view of the opening ceremony of the Fourth Session of the 14th National People's Congress of China at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing, China, 05 March 2026. China's major annual political meetings, known as the 'Lianghui' or 'Two Sessions,' began on 04 March with the opening of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), while the National People's Congress (NPC) opens on 05 March. EPA/ANDRES MARTINEZ CASARES
Following the “Two Sessions” in Beijing, the Macao SAR bodies promised to implement the “spirit” of the ANP and the CPPCC, highlighting above all the predominance of executive power and the safeguarding of national security for the stability of the territory
Macao SAR high-ranking officials promised to implement the “spirit” of the “Two Sessions” after the fourth session of the 14th National People's Assembly and the 14th National Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC). The president of the Court of Final Instance (TUI) began by saying that the persistence of the “one country, two systems” principle is the “fundamental basis and guarantee of long-term prosperity and stability” of Macau; Integration in the country represents “the inevitable direction for the future” of the territory. Song Man Lei also stressed that the judicial bodies and administrative bodies, although with different functions, “share the same fundamental objective, which is to guarantee the general interests of Macau society and the fundamental well-being of its residents”. In this sense, he said that the Macao SAR courts “will exercise judicial power in accordance with the law, guided by the concept of impartial justice, firmly supporting the Chief Executive in his governance (…) and actively cooperating, at the judicial level, with the Macao SAR Government in increasing the efficiency of governance”. In view of the 15th National Five-Year Plan and the 3rd Five-Year Plan of the Macao SAR, Song said that the courts of the three instances “will seriously study and implement the spirit of the ‘Two Sessions’”, focusing on four aspects. First of all, “persist in impartial justice and improve judicial efficiency”, and “firmly safeguard national security, severely punishing, in accordance with the law, crimes against national security”. Thirdly, the president of the TUI highlighted the reinforcement of the training of the team of judges, “cultivating the awareness of defending the sovereignty, security and development interests of the State, ensuring the unity of the political, legal and social effects of the judgment of cases”. Finally, he said that the judicial cooperation mechanism with Mainland China will be improved. Song Man Lei also stated that the courts will continue to safeguard the principle of “one country, two systems”, “cooperating with the various sectors of society in realizing the predominance of executive power”. The Prosecutor, Tong Hio Fong, said that the Public Ministry “will remain faithful to the political structure with the predominance of the executive branch, intensify communication with the executive and legislative bodies, carry out its functions in a fair and impartial manner and process processes rigorously and in compliance with the law”. He also guaranteed that he will increase staff awareness “regarding the inseparable link between national security and the stability and prosperity of the Macao SAR”, as well as “resolutely” combat “illegal and criminal acts that endanger the sovereignty, security and development interests of the State”. The president of the Legislative Assembly (AL), in turn, assured that the Hemicycle will “make every effort” to contribute “proactively” to the implementation of the 15th Five-Year Plan, namely by carrying out “scientific studies on priority work, presenting opinions and suggestions on policies and legislation”, so that there is a “scientific articulation” with the 3rd Plan of the Macao SAR. André Cheong also said that the AL “will take concrete measures to implement the principle of the predominance of executive power, so as not to be just a ‘commentator’ or ‘spectator’”. Specifically, through “internal reforms, it will seek to achieve high-quality political discussion and will make every effort to promote legislative production and supervision”. He also promised that the body he heads will “strengthen coordinated legislative production and promote the connection of rules and mechanisms between Macau and Hengqin”. The five Secretaries also stated that they will implement the “spirit” of the “Two Sessions”, stating that “the predominance of the executive power will be firmly defended”. Special mention goes to the Secretary for Security, who stated that “the safeguarding of national security and social stability constitute the common mission and responsibility of all security personnel”. In this sense, he considers that “it is imperative to understand and implement” the “spirit” of the ANP and the CPPCC. Chan Tsz King also discussed the legislative work on the laws and complementary diplomas relating to the State Security Defense Commission, which he considered “an important step to respond to and integrate the national development plan”. The Commissioners against Corruption and Audit, the director general of the Customs Services and the commander general of the Unitary Police Services also assured that they will study and learn the “spirit” of the “Two Sessions”.
CP.

item.title: Fight against sexist billboards spread across Peninsular Malaysia's highways
item.description:
If you are a Malaysian driving back to your hometown, it will be difficult not to notice the plethora of eye-catching billboards dotted along Peninsular Malaysia's highways. However, some billboards have angered many people by using language that could be considered misogynistic and sexist – from products that exploit skin color sensitivity to health supplements with sexual innuendo. For Malaysian pediatrician Ayne Zarof, one of these billboards outraged her so many years ago that she pushed for it to be removed – and succeeded, after filing a complaint with the regulatory body Advertising Standards Malaysia (ASA). “It was a quick and straightforward process,” Ayne, who now works in the United Kingdom, told the local press. The billboard in question promoted a skin-whitening serum with the slogan “the cause of polygamy”, showing a man in traditional Malaysian wedding attire shielding his eyes with sunglasses from the glare of the serum. “Not only does it make fun of polygamy, it also reduces women to objects,” he said in an Instagram post at the time, stressing that “he insinuates that women attract other people’s husbands.” In Malaysia, Muslim men can marry up to four women under Sharia law, with the approval of the Sharia Court, although the practice remains controversial in society. Admitting she had little hope anything would be done, Dr Ayne was surprised when the offending ad was taken down two weeks later. The authority ruled that the advertisement violated the Malaysian Code of Advertising Practice as it was offensive and derogatory. “I hoped that, with my video on social media, other people could help clean our streets,” he added about his post, which went viral in January 2026. In the video, Ayne said the billboard incident, which made her feel “sick and angry,” happened several years ago, but she decided to share her experience recently in light of the murder of 16-year-old Yap Shing Xuen, who was stabbed to death in October 2025 by a 14-year-old schoolmate. The murder sparked a national discussion about misogyny and violence against women. “It’s clear that our society and the world still have misogynistic views about women,” he said. Ayne Zarof first contacted the company responsible for the ad, stating that there must be a better way to promote the product, but she was referred from person to person, and they all denied any responsibility for the advertising. Despite his experience, the situation did not appear to have improved, even years later. In February 2026, linguistics researcher Miriam Devaprasana shared photographs of promotional posters for the subscription television platform Astro on a light rail (LRT) carriage in Kuala Lumpur, which she said took on a misogynistic tone to promote a drama series. They contained subtitles in Malay that can be translated as “there is no point in a wife being beautiful if she disobeys her husband” and “wives who deny their husbands sex fall into sin and are far from paradise”. Miriam said it was disappointing that Astro, LRT operator RapidKL and the series' producers chose to amplify such messages, saying they overstepped the marketing line and normalized nefarious ideas. “If these ads are intended to generate interest in the series, they do so at a serious social cost,” he reported on Facebook. “Using public infrastructure to disseminate these messages risks undermining decades of ongoing, hard-fought efforts by advocates, activists, educators and communities working for real, tangible change in relation to gender equality,” he added. Astro responded by issuing a statement on its website, stating that the advertisements had now been removed, adding that it did not endorse the views expressed and that the campaign aimed to highlight stereotypes explored in the drama series. “We recognize that presenting them in a public setting without full context may have led to misinterpretations and we unreservedly apologize for any resulting misunderstanding,” the broadcaster said. The proliferation of illegal billboards makes it difficult to enforce regulations. In 2019, more than 450 illegal billboards were identified along the North-South Highway by the Nilai Municipal Council, while the council of neighboring Seremban city reported more than 200 on the same section in 2024. In Petaling Jaya, Mayor Mohamad Zahri Samingon personally stood on a lifting platform to remove an illegal billboard on the federal highway after the owner refused to remove it even after receiving a notice to do so.
JTM with international agencies
